data.title: Euphronios Krater
data.knownHistory: Surfaced on the market in 1971 with no documented prior ownership.

message: Searching authoritative sources (Met, UNESCO, Art Loss Register)…

data.claim: Euphronios Krater — The Met (formerly), repatriated to Italy 2008
data.sourceTitle: Euphronios Krater — The Met (formerly), repatriated to Italy 2008
data.sourceQuote: The Metropolitan Museum of Art acquired the Euphronios (Sarpedon) krater in 1972 for $1 million. Following Italy's evidence of illicit excavation, the Met agreed in 2006 to transfer title; the krater was returned to Italy in January 2008.

data.claim: Sarpedon Krater displayed at Villa Giulia / Cerveteri
data.sourceTitle: Sarpedon Krater displayed at Villa Giulia / Cerveteri
data.sourceQuote: Repatriated antiquities including the Euphronios krater are associated with Etruscan sites near Cerveteri; UNESCO frameworks on illicit trafficking of cultural property (1970 Convention) underpin such returns.

data.claim: Acquisition controversy and the Medici conviction
data.sourceTitle: Acquisition controversy and the Medici conviction
data.sourceQuote: Press and registry coverage tied the krater's surfacing in the early 1970s to dealer networks later subject to looting investigations; provenance before 1971 is undocumented.

message: 9 register(s) checked — 2 possible match(es), 2 not machine-queryable. No register can return "clear".

checks.registry: INTERPOL Stolen Works of Art Database
checks.method: Domain-scoped search of interpol.int. This searches the registry's public WEBSITE, not its register.
checks.coverage: The only global database of certified police information on stolen and missing cultural objects (~52,000 items). Entries come from member-country police forces, so an object only appears if a theft was reported to and recorded by a national force. Unreported and never-inventoried material — most looted archaeological and colonial-era objects — is structurally absent.
checks.caveat: No evidence surfaced through the access available. This is NOT a statement that the object is absent from this register, and it is NOT a clean bill of health — objects looted at source were never inventoried and cannot appear in a stolen-property register at all.

checks.method: No programmatic interface exists. Deep link emitted for a human to run the authoritative search.
checks.coverage: The same Stolen Works of Art database, searchable by image or description from a phone with no login required. This is the fastest authoritative check a human can run on an object in front of them — and it cannot be automated, which is why it appears here as a referral rather than a result.
checks.caveat: This register could not be searched programmatically. Nothing about the object has been established either way — the official search below must be run by a human.

checks.issuer: US Federal Bureau of Investigation — Art Crime Team
checks.method: Domain-scoped search of fbi.gov. This searches the registry's public WEBSITE, not its register.
checks.coverage: Stolen art and cultural property submitted by US and foreign law-enforcement agencies. Entry requires a police report and a value threshold, so it is a record of reported thefts from documented collections, not of looting at source.
checks.caveat: No evidence surfaced through the access available. This is NOT a statement that the object is absent from this register, and it is NOT a clean bill of health — objects looted at source were never inventoried and cannot appear in a stolen-property register at all.

checks.method: Domain-scoped search of carabinieri.it, beniculturali.it. This searches the registry's public WEBSITE, not its register.
checks.coverage: Over 1.1 million stolen or illicitly exported cultural objects — the largest such archive in the world, and the most important single source for Italian antiquities. Public search is a web form; the full archive is law-enforcement access.
checks.caveat: No evidence surfaced through the access available. This is NOT a statement that the object is absent from this register, and it is NOT a clean bill of health — objects looted at source were never inventoried and cannot appear in a stolen-property register at all.

checks.issuer: German Lost Art Foundation (Deutsches Zentrum Kulturgutverluste)
checks.method: Domain-scoped search of lostart.de, kulturgutverluste.de, proveana.de. This searches the registry's public WEBSITE, not its register.
checks.coverage: Cultural property lost, moved or seized as a result of Nazi persecution 1933–1945, plus wartime relocations. Carries both 'found' and 'search' reports. The reference register for Nazi-era provenance gaps — and irrelevant to colonial-era and archaeological looting.
checks.caveat: No evidence surfaced through the access available. This is NOT a statement that the object is absent from this register, and it is NOT a clean bill of health — objects looted at source were never inventoried and cannot appear in a stolen-property register at all.

checks.method: Domain-scoped search of getty.edu. This searches the registry's public WEBSITE, not its register.
checks.coverage: Transcribed auction catalogues, dealer stock books and collection inventories — the primary evidence base for reconstructing an ownership chain rather than for flagging a theft. Strongest on the European and American art market; thin outside it.
checks.caveat: No evidence surfaced through the access available. This is NOT a statement that the object is absent from this register, and it is NOT a clean bill of health — objects looted at source were never inventoried and cannot appear in a stolen-property register at all.

checks.registry: ICOM Red Lists of Cultural Objects at Risk
checks.method: Domain-scoped search of icom.museum, unesco.org. This searches the registry's public WEBSITE, not its register.

hits.claim: Sarpedon Krater displayed at Villa Giulia / Cerveteri
hits.sourceQuote: Repatriated antiquities including the Euphronios krater are associated with Etruscan sites near Cerveteri; UNESCO frameworks on illicit trafficking of cultural property (1970 Convention) underpin such returns.

checks.coverage: Object CATEGORIES at risk of illicit trafficking by region — not individual objects. A Red List hit never identifies a specific piece; it says an object of this type from this region should not move without documentation. That is a due-diligence trigger, not an identification.
checks.caveat: A source on this registry's domain names this object. This is a lead requiring human verification against the register itself, not a confirmed register entry.

checks.issuer: Wikidata contributors — community-maintained, not an authority
checks.method: SPARQL over Q1323051 (Euphronios krater) — significant-event (P793), collection (P195), origin (P495), discovery (P189). Chosen from 3 label-search candidate(s): Q15639446 Euphronios Krater; Q1323051 Euphronios krater; Q48997113 Amazonomachy on the Arezzo 1465 Vase. Entity resolution is shown so the choice can be audited rather than trusted.

hits.claim: significant event: claim for restitution of an artwork
hits.sourceQuote: Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · significant event = claim for restitution of an artwork
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · significant event = erroneous provenance (artwork)
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · significant event = archaeological looting
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · significant event = restitution

hits.claim: collection: Museo Nazionale Etrusco di Villa Giulia
hits.sourceQuote: Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · collection = Museo Nazionale Etrusco di Villa Giulia
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · inception = -0515-01-01T00:00:00Z
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · location = Cerveteri
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · creator = Euxitheos
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · creator = Euphronios
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · location = Museo Nazionale Archeologico
Euphronios krater — ancient Greek bowl used for mixing wine with water, made around 515 B.C. · collection = Metropolitan Museum of Art

checks.coverage: The only openly machine-queryable source here. Its 'significant event' statements carry dated values such as archaeological looting, art theft, restitution and claim for restitution, usually with a reference URL. Coverage is uneven and edits are unreviewed, so this generates leads to verify — never findings.
checks.caveat: Wikidata is community-maintained and certifies nothing. Dated events here are leads to verify against the cited source, not findings.

checks.issuer: The Art Loss Register (commercial due-diligence registry)
checks.method: Commercial register — reached through the agent's paid x402 check, not this free layer.
checks.coverage: The largest private database of stolen and looted art. Registration and search are commercial services — which is exactly why this one is the agent's paid x402 check rather than a free lookup.
checks.caveat: This register could not be searched programmatically. Nothing about the object has been established either way — the official search below must be run by a human.

redFlags.evidence: Authoritative sources show an undocumented period in the ownership timeline.
Linked to italy — a UNESCO 1970 source country with active repatriation claims.
Sources contain looting / repatriation language associated with this object or its type.

Register source names this object in theft/looting/restitution terms: “Sarpedon Krater displayed at Villa Giulia / Cerveteri” (https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/). Lead requiring verification against the register itself.

Register source names this object in theft/looting/restitution terms: “significant event: claim for restitution of an artwork” (https://www.ifar.org/case_summary.php?docid=1184619726). Lead requiring verification against the register itself.

Register source names this object in theft/looting/restitution terms: “significant event: erroneous provenance (artwork)” (https://soundcloud.com/vamuseum/s2-e7-youve-gotta-have-good). Lead requiring verification against the register itself.

2 of 9 registers could not be searched programmatically (no public API — INTERPOL, FBI NSAF and Carabinieri TPC among them). Their absence from these results carries no information; run the official searches by hand.
Asking $1,200,000 is 4.8× the ~$250,000 market estimate — possible value laundering.
Art Loss Register premium search: MATCH: object family appears in repatriation/stolen records (MOCK fixture).

data.rationale: 8 red flag(s) detected from authoritative sources; confidence reduced accordingly.

identifyingRegisters.name: INTERPOL Stolen Works of Art
identifyingRegisters.requires: a theft reported to and recorded by a member-country police force

identifyingRegisters.name: INTERPOL ID-Art
identifyingRegisters.requires: the same police report as the Stolen Works of Art database

identifyingRegisters.name: Carabinieri TPC
identifyingRegisters.requires: an Italian nexus — the archive is national

weakRegisters.name: ICOM Red Lists
weakRegisters.why: Lists object categories at risk, never individual objects — a due-diligence trigger, not an identification.

weakRegisters.name: Wikidata
weakRegisters.why: Coverage follows scholarly and press attention rather than rule, so its silence carries little information.

blindRegisters.name: FBI National Stolen Art File
blindRegisters.why: Holds market-theft losses; requires a law-enforcement submission and a value threshold.

blindRegisters.name: Lost Art Database
blindRegisters.why: Holds nazi-era losses; requires a loss connected to Nazi persecution, 1933–45.

blindRegisters.name: Getty Provenance Index
blindRegisters.why: Holds market, market-theft, nazi-era losses; requires passage through the European or American art market.

blindRegisters.name: Art Loss Register
blindRegisters.why: Holds market-theft, nazi-era losses; requires a registered loss, usually by an owner or insurer.

coverage.note: 3 of 9 registers could systematically name this object. Silence here is comparatively informative: records would be expected to exist.
coverage.comparability: This score is comparable only to other well covered objects. Comparison against a structurally uncovered object will understate how little was checked there.
